California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
August 23, 
■99■
LOis Stornetta
Byron― Bethany lrrigation District
3944 Main Street
P.0. Box 273
Byron′  cA  945■4
Dear Us. Stornetta:
You have requested advice on behalf of Byron-Bethany
Irrigation District director CharLes Uznay concerning the
application of the conf lj'ct-of- inlerest provisions of the 
-
poiiticaf 
Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Because your request does not
seek guidance about a specific pending decision, !/e are treating
your iequest as one for informai assiitance-2
OUEST ION
If the effect of Director Uznay's decision on his economic
interest is not distinguishable fron its effect on the public
generally, wiII he have a conflict of interest?
1 Gor...,..nt code Sections 81ooo-91-015. Al'1 statutory references
are to the covernnent code unless otherwise indicated. 
Cornmission
regulations appear at 2 catifornia code of Regulations section
18ooo, et seo. A1t references to regulations are to Title 2,
Dj,vision 6 of the california code of Regulations.
You informed us that you r.rere naking the request on behalf of
Director Uznay in our telephone conversation of August 20, 199L.
2 
Irrforral- assistance does not provide the requestor r.rith the
imrnunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329 (c) (3) . )
Re:
Your Request For AdViCe
Our File No. I-91-■
83
428 J Street,Suite 800 0 P.0.Box 807 0 Sacramcnto CA 95804´0807 ● (916)322・5660


Our File NO. ■-9■―■83
Page 2
CONCLUS ION
If the effect of Director Uznay's decision on his economic
interest is not distinguishable fron its effect on the public
generally, there is no conflict of interest.
ANALYSIS
Section 8710o prohibits any public official 
frorn making,
parti-ipating in maiing, or othLrwise.using his official 
position
io influence a governrnentat decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest.
The Act provides a four-part test to determine whether a
public officiil 
has a conflict of interest in a governmental
t""iri"". 
First, is the official 
making, participating in naking'
or using his official 
position to influence a qovernBentpL.
J""iii""z 
(section 87ioo.) second, is it foreseeable that the
a;ision wiil affect the oificial,s 
economic interests? . (Section
;tio,:t 
inira, is the effect of the decision on the officiays
econonic interests material? (Id.) 
Fourth, is the effect of the
a""irion on the orricial,s 
econonic interests distinquishable from
its effect on the public qenerallv?. (Id') 
Each of these elements
ffihere 
is a conflict of interest. 
(The tests
aie s"t forth in the Commission,s pamphlet, "California's Conflict
of Interest Law for PubIic of f icials,'t t/hich your request makes
reference to. )
Your question concerns the fourth elenent of the test' 
Even
if the decision before a public official 
neets aII the other three
eiements, disqua 1i f ication is required only if the - financial
effect upon tlie officialrs 
econonic interests is distinguishable
fron the effect upon the Public generally. 
(Section 87103') The
,'!uU1ic" is aII tie persons residing, owning property, or -doing
birsiness in the jurisdiction of the agency in question. (In re
Leqan (1985) 9 FiPc Ops. 1, 15.) In the case of a director of a
r.rater aistrict, 
this lrould be the entire district- 
If the
decision does not affect aII the menbers of the public in the same
manner, disqua I ification nay be required unless the effect of the
decision is the same as the effect on a significant seqment of the
public. 
(Regulation 18703.) Therefore, for the t'public
generallyrt exception to apply, any decision would have to affect
at teast a significant segTnent of persons in the Byron-Bethany
Irrigation District in substantially the sane manner as it would
affect Director Uznay. (Doud Advice Letter, No. A-88-214; Burnham
Advice Letter, No. A-86-210. )
The coEDission has never adopted a strict arithnetic test for
deternining uhat constitutes a significant segnent of the public.
Horrever, in order to apply the 'rpublic generallytr exceptj.on, the
population affected nust be large in number and heterogeneous in
nature. (In re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops 62; Flvnn Advice Letter,
No. r-88-430. )
We have advised in the past that 36 percent of the housing
units and population of a county constituted a significant segment


Our FiIe No. I-91-18 3
Page 3
of the public. 
(Marsh Advice Letter, No I-9o-151.) we have
advised that the 25 percent of a city's population served by a ne$,
bridge was a significant segment of the population. (Christensen
Advice Letter, No. A-89-422. ) 
We have also advised that two
percent of the sinilarly situated homeor.rners and one Percent of
Lhe population of a city's population are not a significant
segiment of the pubtic. 
(Remelnever Advice Letter, No. 87-210;
zarnboni Advice Letter, No. A-89-021.) The residential units in a
developnent zone constituting five percent of the residences in a
city is not a significant segment of the population. 
(cosqrove
Advice Letter, No. A-89-120.) We have also said that 15 land
owners out of the entire city of carlsbad was not a significant
segment of the population of carlsbad. (Biondo Advice Letter, No.
r-90-241. )
As you have mentioned no specific decision, we cannot advise
whether or not the "public generally" or "significant segrent of
the publict' exception appties. As decisions arise, Director Uznay
should: (1) examine the effect of the decision on his business,
real property, or other economic interests, (2) deterrnine how many
other people in the district are affected in the sane manner as
he, and (3) compare the number of people affected in the sarne
manner as he with atl the people in the district. 
The analysis
requires that there be at least a reasonable, good faith showing
of the conmon irnpact of the particular governnental decision.
(Green Advice Letter, No. A-90-075.)
If you have any further questions regarding this natter,
please contact ne at (916) 322-5901.5
Sincerely,
Scott HalLabrin
Acting ceneraL counsel

a cost of 10C per page.

interest is not distinguishable fron its effect on the public
generally, there is no conflict of interest.
